card.title: The Immortal Jellyfish
card.teaser: There's an animal no bigger than your fingernail that can hit the reset button on its own life — as many times as it wants.
card.coverImagePrompt: A tiny translucent jellyfish drifting in dark ocean water, bioluminescent, glowing pink and violet, shot with cinematic backlight, macro underwater photography

lessons.title: A creature that refuses to die
lessons.body: Most animals follow a one-way arrow: birth, growth, aging, death. But a jellyfish the size of a pinhead seems to have discovered a back door. When it gets injured, starved, or simply old, it can melt back into a blob, reattach to a rock, and start over from infancy — potentially forever.

visual.imagePrompt: A small Turritopsis jellyfish slowly dissolving into a cocoon-like blob on a rock, otherworldly, deep sea aesthetic

lessons.title: Meet Turritopsis dohrnii
lessons.body: Turritopsis dohrnii is a species of jellyfish barely 4.5 millimeters across, native to warm Mediterranean and Pacific waters. Most jellyfish reproduce, drift off, and die within a few months. Turritopsis cheats. When stressed, it performs a biological trick nicknamed 'transdifferentiation' — its adult cells actually rewind into younger cell types.

keyTerms.definition: When one type of mature cell transforms directly into a different mature cell type, skipping the usual stem-cell stage
The juvenile, attached stage of a jellyfish's life cycle

visual.imagePrompt: A microscopic view of Turritopsis dohrnii showing its transparent bell and orange stomach visible inside

lessons.title: How it rewinds
lessons.body: When an adult Turritopsis is threatened, its bell collapses and its tentacles retract. Over a few days, the whole animal reabsorbs into a little cyst on the seafloor and reorganizes itself back into a polyp — the juvenile, plant-like stage. From that polyp, it can bud off new baby jellyfish that are genetically identical to the 'old' adult. It's the equivalent of a frog reverting into a tadpole and then re-hatching.

visual.imagePrompt: Four-stage diagram of a jellyfish life cycle with an arrow looping backward from adult to polyp, scientific illustration

lessons.title: Discovered by accident
lessons.body: In 1988, a marine biology student named Christian Sommer was raising Turritopsis in jars and noticed the adults kept disappearing — replaced by polyps he hadn't added. At first he assumed he'd mixed up the samples. It took nearly a decade for researchers to confirm that the same animals were turning themselves back into juveniles. A 2022 genome study identified dozens of genes involved in DNA repair and cellular rebooting that are ramped up during the transformation.
lessons.funFact: The species has almost accidentally been spread worldwide in ballast tanks of cargo ships — a slow, quiet 'immortal' invasion.

visual.imagePrompt: A scientist peering into a beaker holding tiny pulsating jellyfish, warm lab light, intrigue on their face

lessons.title: Why 'immortal' has an asterisk
lessons.body: Turritopsis is biologically immortal — not invincible. A fish can still eat it. But in terms of aging alone, it has cracked a problem that has baffled everything else in the animal kingdom. Understanding how its cells rewind may teach us something profound about our own aging — and why most animals have never found the same trick.

quiz.question: What unusual trick does Turritopsis dohrnii perform when stressed?
options: It reverts from an adult back into a juvenile polyp
It regrows missing parts from a severed piece
quiz.explanation: When injured or old, Turritopsis reabsorbs its adult body and reorganizes itself back into a polyp — the juvenile stage.

quiz.question: What is 'transdifferentiation'?
options: The process of eggs becoming fertilized
A mature cell turning directly into a different cell type
The merging of two species through breeding
The act of shedding old cells
quiz.explanation: Transdifferentiation is when a fully mature, specialized cell changes into a different kind of specialized cell — skipping the usual stem-cell middle step.

quiz.question: Why does 'immortal' have an asterisk for Turritopsis dohrnii?
options: The animal can still be eaten or killed
quiz.explanation: The jellyfish is biologically immortal — it doesn't age to death — but it can still be eaten by predators or destroyed by disease.

sources.title: Reversing the Life Cycle: Medusae Transforming into Polyps and Cell Transdifferentiation in Turritopsis nutricula
Comparative genomics of mortal and immortal cnidarians unveils novel keys behind rejuvenation
